[JS Weird World] No.48 Everything Is an Object (or a Primitive)
Now that we understand object prototypes, we can dig into one more thing: in JavaScript, everything is either an object or a primitive.
Numbers, booleans, strings, functions, arrays, objects — they all have a prototype. Everything except the base object.
Everything Is an Object (or a Primitive)
Let's work through this with an example. I'll use these three things — an object, a function, and an array — to show why everything has a prototype.
First, try pasting that into the browser console, then type
You get a base object. This sits very low on the prototype chain, and it has properties and methods of its own.
What about the function? Try typing
That's the prototype shared by all functions. Every function you create has this prototype, along with its related properties and methods (you'll spot some familiar ones —
That's the array prototype. Let's check whether it has properties and methods too (and again, you'll recognize a lot of these).
So from these three examples, we can pin down one thing:
Every object, every array, and every function in JavaScript has a prototype.
Now here's a fun question — what's the prototype of a prototype?
It's a prototype. So keep this in mind: the thing sitting at the very bottom of the prototype chain is the prototype object.

Why do people say everything in JavaScript is an object? Arrays have a prototype, functions have a prototype, and even numbers and strings do too. In this post we follow __proto__ all the way down the prototype chain to the base object sitting at the very bottom.
